summary: This is a memorial tribute to John Kordyback, a Thoughtworks colleague who died at 64. The post celebrates his 20+ year career helping organizations adopt agile practices, particularly his pioneering work applying continuous delivery to legacy mainframe systems. Fowler and numerous colleagues share memories emphasizing John's exceptional kindness, mentorship abilities, and understanding that agile success depends fundamentally on human relationships. The tributes consistently highlight his patient, empathetic approach to consulting, his infectious humor, and his ability to build trust with everyone from junior developers to executives.
key_points: John specialized in the challenging work of applying agile and continuous delivery practices to legacy mainframe systems, recognizing that 'legacies' are valuable precisely because they run critical business processes
He understood that agile success comes from humans working together, not from frameworks or certifications - 'the most important thing is to get people talking to each other'
His consulting approach combined deep technical knowledge with genuine empathy, listening to existing mainframe teams rather than imposing solutions that wouldn't work in their context
Multiple colleagues independently emphasize his extraordinary kindness, patience, and mentorship as his defining characteristics
He faced terminal cancer with the same positive, graceful attitude he brought to difficult technical challenges
money_quotes: He recognized that these were called legacies because they were valuable to their recipients.
The core principle of agile that John understood was that agile habits were founded upon humans working together.
Don't worry about that stuff. The most important thing is to get people talking to each other.
His goal was much greater than agile, it was to bring decency and happiness to people's working lives.
A significant professional life is not a once-and-done affair. Every engagement is first and foremost an engagement with a fresh group of unique persons.
Software is a social activity, and change is often best rooted in day-to-day activities and habits, in trust, and can be most enduring when it emerges from the ground up.
You can do anything with Excel if you really have to.
key_insight: Technical excellence in software development is inseparable from human kindness and the ability to build genuine relationships with the people you work alongside.
